Thanks for taking the time to pull me in @uiri. I meant to respond to your original comments @ryanhiebert but that somehow slipped off the checklist.

So I completely agree with the removal of the `__init__` declaration that was an oversight.

As for using an OrderedDict for _InlineTableDict, this current implementation doesn't work with Python 2.6. We're maintaining support for 2.6 for the immediate future in `pipenv` unfortunately, so if this is merged, we'll either need to fork or find another solution for toml parsing.

This is also adding functionality, which is probably fine, but will change how this library has operated for most of its existence. I'm not entirely convinced that `OrderedDict` is a superior choice over `dict` when the user can pass any custom `Mapping` class to `_dict`.

I think if `toml` wants to support custom mappings for Inline Tables, the ideal option here is to have InlineTableDict inherit from the value of `_dict`. That's a much more complicated patch though, but would be consistent. Otherwise, this makes a breaking change to move from one static type to another, lesser used, static type.

That's just my 2 cents though, feel free to do what you feel is best for the library and we'll cope. Thanks for your work @uiri and @ryanhiebert!

Thanks for reporting this.

I can move those to their own file that uses a fixture to skip them and will only turn them on if an environment variable is set. The reason for doing that is because those tests are there to explicitly exercise the full stack (all the way down into the real sockets).

Does that seem like a reasonable solution? I've done a similar thing in wsgi-intercept to avoid real network contact for packaging contexts.

What follows is some additional commentary on the nature of gabbi which you can safely ignore if you're not all that interested, but might be usefu:

Note that gabbi tests are not intended to be unit tests and not intended to operate in isolation. Each file is an ordered suite. Any setup (for fixtures) is done at the level of the yaml file, not per test. The tests before any one test impact that test, so you can build up a collection of resources or tell an api "story" in one file.

The tests you list above are part of gabbi using gabbi to test itself. There are other tests within the tests dir which are more explicitly unit tests (some even use mocks).

Pretty much the entire reason that gabbi exists is because mocks are a bad idea when trying to do full throated tests of HTTP APIs. The combination of gabbi and wsgi-intercept means that you're testing a real wsgi application, through real http clients, and sending back and forth real HTTP, not faking it.

> This is the type of things that are really annoying about the unicode/str story.

I swear an awful lot of these issues would disappear if Python (2 and 3) defaulted to UTF-8 instead of ASCII.

> which makes a unicode object in Python2

It already is a Unicode object thanks to `unicode_literals`. I also tried using `b'default_en.txt'`, but `setuptools` throws an exception either way.

It looks like it would require a fair bit of refactoring to get `pint` to work with non-ASCII paths in Python 2. In addition to `pkg_resources`, the parser also throws a `UnicodeDecodeError` when it reaches an `@import` statement, as the file contents have already been decoded by that point.

I'm not sure it's worth addressing the issue, tbh. It should only ever arise when someone has bundled `pint` instead of installing it, and in that case, they must also know where `default_en.txt` is in relation to their own code, so they can explicitly pass a Unicode path to `UnitRegistry()`.

It's documented now in this issue. I reckon that's enough.

On Jun 27, 2018 9:19 PM, "Mahmoud Hashemi" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

OK cool, looking at that last change, this is getting pretty close to
shippable. I'm pretty sure there's a Gödelian issue/feature we can make by
maintaining the invariant that S[T] != S, but instead T continues to
reference its previous value. The motivation behind this is twofold:

   - You already have S, so S[T] equaling S kind of doesn't add anything.
   - You can continue iterating while referring to the scope, so: [(S,
   T.method(S['some_context']))] continues to work. This probably isn't a
   very common pattern, but for us, and some future subset of power users,
   this pattern will help avoid having to write extensions and reduce
   extraneous Call usage.

Does that make sense?

When solving this issue, keep in mind the following interesting property from Sinon.JS (basically, the replacement function becomes the default, but it can still be overridden by _e.g._ withArgs):

```javascript
o = {func: function(x) {return x*2;}}
stub = sinon.stub(o, 'func').callsFake(function(x) {return 'Hello World!';})
stub.withArgs('A').returns('Arg of A')
stub() // "Hello World!"
stub('A') // "Arg of A"
stub.returns(5)
stub() // 5
stub('A') // "Arg of A"
```

Hi @dlasusa, you've managed to hit a bit of an obscure edge case which will need to be fixed. Short answer, reassign the field to the list:

```python
ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=200)
field = ticket.custom_fields[0]
field['value'] = "SOMEVALUE"
ticket.custom_fields[0] = field
zenpy_client.tickets.update(ticket)
```

It's nasty, it's dumb, it shouldn't be that way, but it will work for now.

Long answer, it has to do with incremental object updates. Basically, Zenpy used to send all the attributes of an object to Zendesk to be updated, even if they had not changed. The assumption was that updating an item with the same value is essentially a NOP. This is true, but the way it plays out in the real world is something like this:

* Zenpy pulls ticket N
* some other process P modifies ticket N
* Zenpy executes an update to ticket N
*  process P's change to ticket N is reverted

To solve this problem Zenpy was patched to only send those attributes that have been modified to Zendesk when executing an update. This was simple enough for direct changes to a Zenpy object, for example Ticket.status, but a bit trickier when that attribute is a list or dictionary. How do you know whether or not the dict or list was modified? To solve that issue, I implemented proxy lists and dicts, so I can detect whether or not a list or dict has been altered:

```python
>>> ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=200)
>>> print(type(ticket.custom_fields))
<class 'zenpy.lib.proxy.ProxyList'>
```
The ProxyList implementation has a flaw, in that it does not detect when an item stored in the list has been modified, only whether or not the list was modified, and that explains the behavior you are seeing. I'll need to figure out a way to fix this.

You can see what will be sent to Zendesk in an update as follows:

```python
>>> ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=200)
>>> print(ticket.to_dict(serialize=True))
{'id': 200}
>>> ticket.status = "closed"
>>> print(ticket.to_dict(serialize=True))
{'id': 200, 'status': 'closed'}
```

Hope this helps.

Actually I had an idea for a general solution which may solve this problem. It would be to attach a generator to a fileset. I was inspired by the coregen provider. As far as I can see this provider conflicts with the git provider for example so coregen cores can't be put on git.
What I thought about is to change the following:

    [fileset rom]
    files =
     syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.vhd
     syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.mif[file_type=user]
    file_type = vhdlSource
    
    [provider]
    name = coregen
    script_file = syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.xco
    project_file = syn/xilinx/coregen/Atlys.cgp
    extra_files = bench/asm/TestBench.coe

to

    [fileset rom]
    files =
     syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.vhd
     syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.mif[file_type=user]
    file_type = vhdlSource
    generator = coregen
    script_file = syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.xco
    project_file = syn/xilinx/coregen/Atlys.cgp
    extra_files = bench/asm/TestBench.coe

This way several coregen files can be combined in one core; or several DPI libraries.
In this case the output files would not be stored next to the core; the coregen generator would ask the provider for syn/xilinx/coregen/ROM80.xco, syn/xilinx/coregen/Atlys.cgp, bench/asm/TestBench.coe and generate the files from it.

Default generator would be copy which copies the files retrieved from the provider.

What I did not think through yet is wether or how to connect output files from one generator as input to another generator.

@parkerlreed I've just pushed a working pulseaudio/sbc implementation. It's pretty rough and still has some debug print statements littered throughout.

Getting some of this stuff working nicely is a bit painful. Python threading and multiprocessing has a number of painful limitations so it works in Windows and Linux, which I don't really care about here. I started out trying to use the data management bits from those modules, but os.pipe does a muuuch better job than anything I could put together with them.

Another is that writing to the hidraw output on my system sometimes hangs, so I need some way of interrupting a system call. Python only allows signal/setitimer to be set on the main thread. I've managed to add a gross workaround here using the multiprocessing Pool. I've opted not to use a pure Process, since these sometimes die unexpectedly and a Pool reboots its worker thread automatically.

I think the audio timeout needs some tweaking as well. I'm not sure of the best way of getting frame time length, since the result from sbc_get_frame_duration seems to lie on my system.

I think SWIG might be overkill here too. I can probably make the pulseaudio class an opaque type and make new/start/stop/add_fd/remove_fd functions with C linkage and just wrap those. I should probably also wrap sbc.h instead of the sbc header parser I wrote from following the docs.

Hi, thanks for posting the question. The durable_rules engine runs asynchronously. That is, the outcome `0` returned when asserting a fact or posting an event means the data has been accepted for processing. One thing you can do is store the result in the context state and retrieve the state periodically. For instance:  

1. Run the rule below:

```python
from durable.lang import *

with ruleset('test'):
    # antecedent
    @when_all(m.subject == 'World')
    def say_hello(c):
        # consequent
        print('Hello {0}'.format(c.m.subject))
        c.s.result = 'processed'

run_all()
```
2. Post an event:  
`curl -H "Content-type: application/json" -X POST -d '{"subject": "World"}' http://localhost:5000/test/events`  
3. Retrieve the context state:
`curl http://localhost:5000/test/state`  

You should see: 
`{"sid": "0", "result": "processed"}`  

For more info on the meaning of the outcome code please see the link below:

https://github.com/jruizgit/rules/blob/master/docs/py/reference.md#error-codes

When you run RPIO.cleanup_tcpsockets() on a RPi Model B+ running Python 3.4 then you get an error message about your dictionary changing sizes during iterations (see error message below). I think the issue was because keys() now returns an iterable instead of a list in Python 3, so I just wrapped list around it to get a static list that wouldn't change size. I tested it and it doesn't interfere with the DMA PWM stuff at least, and I don't get thrown the error anymore. This would decrease performance only a tiny bit anyways as a type safety issue.
Best,

I'm not so keen on packing metadata into strings. @dalcinl [proposed](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/issues/175#issuecomment-361695310) more structured tags, which generally seems more Pythonic.  

Another idea is to store the metadata hierarchically.  What's in master at the moment is hierarchical, where in `cell_data`, each type of cell (`'vertex'`, `'line'`, …) has a dict with keys `'physical`', `'geometric'`.  Here in #175 we're talking about inverting this, similar to the discussion [‘tags in VTK’](https://www.vtk.org/pipermail/vtkusers/2017-March/098104.html) with Andy Bauer cited [above](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/issues/175#issuecomment-361887775), but a hierarchy could be used that way too; e.g. (without having thought this through), in `field_data`, instead of a flat dict keyed by user-level names of regions (as in master), put a `'physical'` key there (and `'geometrical'` if desired, though I agree with the [assessment](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/issues/175#issuecomment-361701730) of @dalcinl that they're ‘rather useless’).

The file sender sends buffer-size bytes and waits for an ack. The file receiver waits for buffer-size bytes and sends an ack. There is no retry logic or timeouts, so if a character gets dropped because the serial buffers aren't big enough, then that's what causes the hang.

The 0x52 request is the "Read Tag" request.  0x4C is the "Read Tag Fragmented" request.  By default, if you don't supply an offset, we'll just use Read Tag.  Evidently, some Rockwell devices don't implement it.

To force "Read Tag Fragmented", use the '--fragment' command-line option, or supply an offset:

    python -m cpppo.server.enip --fragment 'my_array[0]'
    python -m cpppo.server.enip 'my_array[0]+0'

Either way will work.

Here's an example of hierarchical segment plots, comparing two annotations from salami:
```python
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
ax = plt.subplot(2,1,1)
mir_eval.display.labeled_intervals(int2, lab2, ax=ax, alpha=0.8, label='small')
mir_eval.display.labeled_intervals(int1, lab1, ax=ax, alpha=0.8, label='functions')
plt.legend(loc='best')
plt.title('Annotator 1')


ax = plt.subplot(2,1,2)
mir_eval.display.labeled_intervals(int4, lab4, ax=ax, alpha=0.8, label='small')
mir_eval.display.labeled_intervals(int3, lab3, ax=ax, alpha=0.8, label='functions')
plt.title('Annotator 2')
plt.legend(loc='best')
```

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1190540/15651692/34073438-264f-11e6-85c1-f51d20463bbc.png)

I think I generally prefer this to the analogous MSAF-style plot:

```python
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
ax = plt.subplot(2,1,1)
mir_eval.display.segments(int1, lab1, base=0.5, ax=ax, text=True)
mir_eval.display.segments(int2, lab2, height=0.5, ax=ax, text=True)
plt.title('Annotator 1')

ax = plt.subplot(2,1,2)
mir_eval.display.segments(int3, lab3, base=0.5, ax=ax, text=True)
mir_eval.display.segments(int4, lab4, height=0.5, ax=ax, text=True)
plt.title('Annotator 2')
```
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1190540/15651776/8d9eeb30-264f-11e6-869d-2301e30c7f01.png)

The first one is a little easier to read (IMO) for a few reasons:

1. no reuse of colors between segment levels
2. color can be used to differentiate levels
3. ability to set legend entries (`small` and `functions`)for different levels
4. spatial patterns between levels are easier to discern than the color patterns in the bottom plot.

The output from the "CB1" and "CB2" submissions had time stamps starting at 0.0, while the mirex ground truth (and all other submissions) have time stamps starting at 0.01. ```python
import telepot

def handler(msg):
    chat_id = msg['chat']['id']
    chat_message = msg['text']
    
    main_keyboard = [['Option #1', 'Option #2'], ['Option #3', 'Option #4']]

The `colorama.Fore.*` and `colorama.Back.*` values are shortcuts to help generate ANSI sequences (see [ANSI escape codes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code)). For example:
```python
>>> colorama.Fore.CYAN
'\x1b[36m'
```
Unix terminals understand these special ANSI codes and will color text when you print them. On Windows, colorama catches these codes and converts them to Windows API calls. The [README](https://github.com/tartley/colorama/blob/master/README.rst) has some more information.

discord.py is an asyncio-based library and therefore things running beside it must either also be asyncio-compatible, running on the same event loop, or in a different thread.

In your example code, the issue is that when you run `run(host='localhost', port=1212)` the thread blocks in order to serve your server content and therefore won't make it to the point where you start up your event loop (and therefore your bot) until the server dies.

Both Flask and Bottle don't appear to be asyncio-compatible by default (although Bottle [appears to have some experimental implementation](https://github.com/bottlepy/bottle/blob/master/bottle.py#L3499). You'd have to use them on another thread in order for them to operate properly (note that by default this creates single-threaded servers that can only handle 1 request at once).

The best solution for this would either be to find equivalent asyncio-compatible libraries or to separate your server and bot into different scripts that push/pull data from a database or websocket.

Thank you for the background info. Yes, I was thinking of submodules.

I didn't realize that the other new repos were from existing projects, but it's actually because `git clone` is not a good distribution method for end users that I think it's convenient to have everything in a single repo. So, anyone that wants to work on the library gets everything with a single `git clone`. Then, from the single repo, one can build any number of products for end users and make those available through distribution channels such as pip, other package managers or just GitHub Releases.

In a single repo approach, you could still keep the older repos in place in order to provide a link to the new home, and you could distribute stuff via Releases on the old repos.

It could be that I'm just being too lazy :) The motivation is simply to not have to put something together from multiple pieces in order to spin up a dev env for one of the components in the library and getting the test suite to run. I haven't actually tried working with the new project layout for luma yet, so I don't know how much of a concern it really is.

Of course, having a single repo also makes it so that there's only a single place for issues, PRs, etc, which may or may not be a good thing...

I was playing around in my head with a number of ideas. I think something like this would be the best implementation.

```python
from sanic import Sanic, Blueprint
from sanic_jwt import Authentication, Configuration  # fka SanicJWTAuthentication

class MyConfig(Configuration):
    COOKIE_SET = True
    URL_PREFIX = '/my_authentication'
    CLAIM_IAT = True

class Initialize(object):
    authentication_class=Authentication
    configuration=MyConfig

    async def authenticate(request, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

    async def store_refresh_token(request, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

    async def retrieve_refresh_token(request, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

    async def retrieve_user(request, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

app = Sanic()
Initialize(app)

# OR

bp = Blueprint('my_authentication_blueprint')
Initialize(bp, CLAIM_IAT=False)
```

Sanic JWT being able to be initialized on an app, or a blueprint. With a customizable `Authentication` and `Configuration`. Settings could be placed:

- As normal dictated by [Sanic documentation](http://sanic.readthedocs.io/en/latest/sanic/config.html),
- Inside a `Configuration` object
- On the instance itself of the `Initialize` object

To accomplish this, I would also suggest we continue leaving the `initialize` method for its simple footprint and (although this is not exactly a big package with a lot of installs) no reason to break backwards compatibility.

> If I am to offer changes improving loader efficiency, what guidelines do I need to adhere to in ensuring API stability?

I think the only two somewhat complicated requirements are not adding any 3rd party dependencies and keeping support for the currently supported python versions. Other than that it should be fine if the unit tests still pass (assuming that you apply the same rigor as you do when working on scikit-learn) and the very simple dict format is returned.

> Is load/loads/ArffDecoder.decode the only real public interface for loading?

Yes.

> Would restructuring the type conversion utilities be welcome?

Yes. They are not facing the public so there's no need to keep them similar.

As especially the first requirement is somewhat odd (not adding any 3rd party libraries such as numpy and scipy), if you want to add 3rd party libraries, I'd be happy to discuss how to move to a new version 3 of liac-arff which is loosens this requirement.

Also, if you do larger changes, please let me know before as I will then prioritize finishing #67 to avoid merge conflicts.

> I am worried about performance, though. The numpy import might incur a big overhead on every read/write. Did you check if this has any measurable performance impact?

I did not measure this, but importing an already imported module should only add the overhead of a (successful) dictionary lookup of `sys.modules['numpy']`.
I'm quite sure that's negligible since it doesn't happen in a tight loop.
In the worst case it will happen once per block in block-based processing and I daresay the additional dictionary lookup will not be a problem.

An alternative (but not strictly equivalent) approach would be to `try` to `import numpy` at module scope and check for an `ImportError`.
Then we could set an internal flag and raise an error if NumPy would be needed but is not available.

But I think that's much worse, because a user might have NumPy installed but might not *want* to use it (e.g. to avoid the load time), which wouldn't be possible with the alternative approach.
I think it would also make the code uglier.

> Also, I think we should keep numpy as a dependency. For most people, pysoundfile is not useful without numpy. Can you think of any other way to handle this?

Hmmm, I only see 3 possible kinds of dependency:

* hard dependency with `install_requires`

* optional dependency with [`extras_require`](https://pythonhosted.org/setuptools/setuptools.html#declaring-extras-optional-features-with-their-own-dependencies) (which is my suggestion)

* no dependency at all

Is there another way?

If we keep it as hard dependency, people without NumPy will not be able to `pip install PySoundFile`, which would make this whole PR more or less useless.

OTOH, installing NumPy with pip will not work on most systems anyway (at least that's my experience). And even if it works, it will most likely be a inferior installation (with regards to the usage of optimized libraries) compared to an installation with a package manager.

I think most users will use a Python system which already has NumPy installed (like Anaconda) or they should use some other package manager to install NumPy anyway.
In the rare cases where they actually want to install with pip, they can use `pip install PySoundFile[numpy]`.

For the Debian package, we could change the dependency from `Depends` to `Recommends`, which would mean in a default setting, NumPy would be installed automatically.

Here's a Debug subclass of the Api if you want to get the raw response bodies for getting testing data (github won't let me just upload the .py file): 

```python
from twitter import Api, TwitterError
import requests


class DebugAPI(Api):
    def DebugEndpoint(self, verb=None, endpoint=None, data=None):
        """ Request a url and return raw data. For testing purposes only.

        Args:
            url:
                The web location we want to retrieve.
            verb:
                Either POST or GET.
            data:
                A dict of (str, unicode) key/value pairs.

        Returns:
            data
        """

        url = "{0}{1}".format(self.base_url, endpoint)

        if verb == 'POST':
            if 'media_ids' in data:
                url = self._BuildUrl(
                    url,
                    extra_params={
                        'media_ids': data['media_ids']
                    }
                )
                print('POSTing url:', url)
            if 'media' in data:
                try:
                    print('POSTing url:', url)
                    raw_data = requests.post(
                        url,
                        files=data,
                        auth=self.__auth,
                        timeout=self._timeout
                    )
                except requests.RequestException as e:
                    raise TwitterError(str(e))
            else:
                try:
                    print('POSTing url:', url)
                    raw_data = requests.post(
                        url,
                        data=data,
                        auth=self.__auth,
                        timeout=self._timeout
                    )
                except requests.RequestException as e:
                    raise TwitterError(str(e))
        if verb == 'GET':
            url = self._BuildUrl(url, extra_params=data)
            print('GETting url:', url)
            try:
                raw_data = requests.get(
                    url,
                    auth=self.__auth,
                    timeout=self._timeout)

            except requests.RequestException as e:
                raise TwitterError(str(e))
        return raw_data._content
```

> Also relevant are instrument name and program name meta-events, as well as channel marker meta-events for type 0 MIDI files.

Incidentally, the instrument name and program name meta-events are [arguably not useful without a channel prefix meta-event](http://www.recordingblogs.com/sa/tabid/88/Default.aspx?topic=MIDI+Instrument+Name+meta+message), and as far as I can tell it's pretty common for MIDI files "in the wild" to omit the channel prefix meta-event (a sampling is copied below).  When they are present, the seem to be less than helpful (like most MIDI text events...)  So, unless someone has a major objection, I am going to only store track name meta-events for now.  This also simplifies the code somewhat.  Using the code
```Python
    for f in glob.glob('data/unique_midi/mid/1/*.mid'):
        try:
            m = midi.read_midifile(f)
        except:
            pass
        for t in m:
            cp = None
            for n, e in enumerate(t):
                if isinstance(e, midi.ChannelPrefixEvent):
                    cp = e.data[0]
                elif isinstance(e, midi.InstrumentNameEvent):
                    if cp is None:
                        print '    Got instrument name "{}" with no channel prefix'.format(e.text)
                    else:
                        print 'Got instrument name "{}" on channel {}'.format(e.text, cp)
                elif isinstance(e, midi.ProgramNameEvent):
                    if cp is None:
                        print '    Got program name "{}" with no channel prefix'.format(e.text)
                    else:
                        print 'Got program name "{}" on channel {}'.format(e.text, cp)
```

The problem with that is dependencies; ffmpeg (or libav) specifically. You can't install ffmpeg from pip, on windows you need to download it separately and add it to your path and on *nix and mac you have to use a package manager.

This would take away the convenience of simply pip installing bandcamp-dl and using it immediately.

However all tracks are already MP3 so re-encoding would only be useful for dropping the quality to reduce filesize, as for FLAC as mentioned before all files are MP3, not much benefit in encoding a lossy file into a lossless format. I can see ogg and wav possibly being useful if you are trying to get the files to be playable with a web player _but its 2018_, [most if not all browsers now support MP3 natively](https://caniuse.com/#feat=mp3).

Its entirely possible to add the feature, I just don't see much point.

I'll leave the request open, if others are interested in it they can chime in and it may get added at some point, bandcamp-dl other than some minor tweaks and a bit of final cleanup is pretty much at its final stage, Id like to keep it as stable as possible for those using it in their own projects.

I have pushed a commit to my fork with expiration tests: https://github.com/whacked/flask-kvsession/commit/c2d972ec464ac36045750ef7e321e63ceee1f129

It's a single new test with a series of assertions testing for session / cookie lifecycle. Some maybe superfluous, but the final assertion is what fails on `master` but passes on my `skip-has_expire-if-TTL-support`. So it depends on what kind of behavior you are expecting regarding client side cookie vs. server side session key.

I should note that this has initially been a feature, not a bug: If a session gets stolen by an attacker and does not have a fixed expiration date, it never expires (because he can prolong it indefinitely). With a fixed expiration, users will have to re-login occasionally, but there won't be any eternal sessions.

On the other hand, this can be worked around by regenerating the session occasionally. It may even be prudent to have two timeouts, one short "keepalive" variant and a longer definite session timeout.

After a little bit of investigation, this isn't related to chartsheets. It also happens with worksheets.

For example, if you try to copy the `Hello 3` string from the workbook generated by this program, you will get the same error:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('first_sheet1.xlsx')

worksheet1 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet2 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet3 = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet3.set_first_sheet()
worksheet3.activate()

worksheet1.write('A1', 'Hello 1')
worksheet3.write('A1', 'Hello 3')

workbook.close()
```
However, it doesn't happen when `set_first_sheet()` isn't the last sheet:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('first_sheet2.xlsx')

worksheet1 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet2 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet3 = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet2.set_first_sheet()  # This line and the filename have changed.
worksheet3.activate()

worksheet1.write('A1', 'Hello 1')
worksheet3.write('A1', 'Hello 3')

workbook.close()
```

So it looks like the "First sheet" shouldn't be the last sheet. Which I guess makes sense.

@dharmen001 I always have the same advice for everyone using conditional formats which is to figure out what you want to do in Excel first and then transfer it to XlsxWriter.

In this case you could probably use a formula that always evaluates to `True` like:

```python
worksheet.conditional_format('A1:A4', {'type':     'formula',
                                       'criteria': '=TRUE',
                                       'format':   your_format})
```

Having said that, this is probably one of those cases where you are pushing the boundaries of the XlsxWriter/Pandas integration and you would be better off just iterating through the dataframe and calling xlsxwriter functions directly to get the formatting that you want. This is more work and initially more error prone but it will give you full control over formatting.

Finally, this isn't a suitable question for "Issues", which should really be bug reports with bug report code. Generally questions are better asked on StackOverflow where questions and answers get more views.

@kurtmckee both sources I've linked to disagree with your position.

The position you have taken, put into words is this:
>"Despite the Atom feed explicitly stating that the content in the element is **Text**, and not entity-encoded html, Feedparser is going to entity-decode it anyway."

The quoted O'reilly book passage from *RSS and Atom Feeds* that I posted from the screenshot spells out fairly clearly how handle text content in Atom feeds, and how you are explicitly not supposed to entity-code text elements.

I'm going to quote another source, an article from xml.org entitled [Handling Atom Text and Content Constructs](http://www.xml.com/pub/a/2005/12/07/handling-atom-text-and-content-constructs.html):

> The problem is that an Atom processor should never second-guess the meaning of the type attribute, and since I implicitly use type="text" a processor will not interpret the contents as markup, as intended for the example.

To me, this is exactly what Feedparser has done, which is second guess the meaning of the type attribute, and what Simplepie has avoided doing.  This is why I still believe that Feedparser is out of spec compliance, and Simplepie is not.  

An argument could be made that Feedparser has made a design decision **to** second guess syntactically valid content, because users are still capable of producing syntactically valid feeds but still make mistakes like putting entity-encoded text in a Text element.  But that doesn't mean that the result Feedparser produced was correct according to the intention of the Atom spec.  

Whether or not that is a valid trade off is left entirely up to you.  I just wanted to point this out to you that based on my reading of the Atom spec, the *RSS and Atom Feeds* book, and the article on handling Atom Text content, it all seemed to be at odds with what Feedparser was producing.

Not sure which part of 1.2.11 caused this change, but the fact that it worked in older versions was probably more of an error/oversight. The code you showed defines the shapefile as POINTZ, but in the point() method it also needed to set the shapeType to POINTZ (the default is POINT). The fact that you specified a z-value didn't change the shapeType (it probably should have). Thus when it saved the file it only wrote the xy values, and hence no z-value when you read it back in. I think version 1.2.3 did not notice when you wrote a shapeType that did not match with the shapefile, but now it raises an exception. 

Nevertheless, since it's easy to forget setting the shapeType argument, as of 0c182098e614e9274e638488a19af7a12674b532 there is a designated method for each shape type, so there is no longer the need for a separate shapeType argument. So in your case, you would simply change `outShp.point(10, 10, 10)` to `outShp.pointz(10, 10, 10)`.

```
which created a Python2-exclusive .whl. By supplying the `--universal` flag, a .whl marked as supporting both versions will be created:
```
$ python setup.py bdist_wheel --universal
$ ls dist
pytest_pycharm-0.4.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
```

libtcod-cffi was merged into tdl.  So the following assumes you'll use the libtcod-cffi API from the tdl package which is documented [here](https://python-tdl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).  The code examples given at the top of most of the tcod sub-module docs should give you an idea of the API additions made.

There are no tutorials for the libtcod-cffi API, and that's a big deal if you're just getting into it.  You could use tdl as a drop in replacement for libtcodpy and use the libtcod tutorials if you think tdl is easier to distribute.  The documentation is mostly complete but often lacks fine details.  If you have any questions about the API then I'll answer them.

Because libtcodpy uses ctypes you don't need to compile the module as a Python C extension, you only need the DLL files.  Of course, you'll still need to get a hold of those DLL's for libtcodpy.  You can find builds for Windows easily, and it's only a little harder to compile some for Linux, but it's difficult to compile for MacOS if you don't have easy access to the platform.  tdl puts compiled extensions for Windows and MacOS on the pypi repository for easy installs, but at the moment there are no longer automated builds for Linux.  tdl's readme will tell you the two commands needed to install tdl on Linux.

libtcodpy is also more compact since it only uses the ctypes module which is already included in Python while tdl needs to install the cffi module and the relatively large numpy modules.

libtcodpy is more or less a direct port of the C API.  For example, if you make an off-screen console then you will need to make a call to free it like in C, and if it goes out of Python's scope before that then you'll have a memory leak.  In tdl all memory handling is been given to Python so that it can free those resources automatically.

tdl has serialization support for every class except Image, which means you can pickle all of those objects.  This feature does not exist in libtcodpy.

There's also a abundance of print_character_at and set_tile_at type functions which do not scale well in Python.  Any performance issues you might have with libtcodpy will be because of the many calls you'll make to these functions.  This is the main reason numpy is included as it lets you access those attributes in large batches.  In tdl, you are given direct access to the memory used by Console objects and several other objects (except Images) which lets you write Python code that runs almost as fast as C code.

I too had this problem and spent way too long debugging it but the following steps worked for me. Especially explicitly resetting the browser cache. 

1. make sure your notebook server isn't running. 
1. run `jt -r`
1. delete the two directories listed by `jt -r`
1.  restart your notebook server
1. (if still not reset) Force your browser to reset its cache. In chrome, you can do this with the devtools open F12. I don't think the Jupyter mini server can track changes to all the dependent files. 
1. if your still having a problems run `curl 'http://localhost:8888/custom/custom.css'` from the command line and manually inspect the css file being returned if it has CSS in it after all these steps you can continue to follow the debugs but at this point you should be all good. 
1. restart your jupyter notebook in debug mode `Jupiter notebook --debug` 
1. at the start of the debug it tried to load configuration from a number of directories depending on you install and package structure. The custom/custom.css should be in one of those directories. Find it and delete it.

You're missing that `author_items` is a QuerySet, not a `list`. So when you do `author_items[n]`, it will fetch at least `n` items, and that's done `inboxlength` times. That's going to hurt when you have 300.000 items in the folder :-)

To do what you're doing, you should do something like this instead:

```python
inbox_length = inbox.total_count  # Will get you the number without counting each item
# values_list(..., flat=True) returns only the requested value in a generator
# .iterator() disables internal caching of the QuerySet, to save some memory
authors = list(inbox.all().values_list('author', flat=True).iterator())
```

Apparently, Outlook does some cool formatting of the binary value. You can do the same in Python (convert to bin to hex and then format without the leading `0x`, and finally upper-case):

```python
>>> import base64
>>> decoded_val = base64.b64decode('AAAAAJXv2ELfyfpHrdn/Vdi/WDYHABw3W1WVDPZHvff3PMvZMf0AAAAWtcAAABw3W1WVDPZHvff3PMvZMf0AABZPjGQAAA==')
>>> ''.join('{:02x}'.format(n) for n in decoded_val).upper()
'0000000095EFD842DFC9FA47ADD9FF55D8BF583607001C375B55950CF647BDF7F73CCBD931FD00000016B5C000001C375B55950CF647BDF7F73CCBD931FD0000164F8C640000'
```

@fmfn It is just binary classification. How does increasing alpha help?

Gaussian processes can naturalize handle noisy regression (when the labels have noise); instead of assigning zero posterior variance to observed points, noisy GP with still show some uncertainty. The noise parameter for a GP with the RBF kernel can be directly interpreted as the expected value of the std variation of noise present in the labels.

Observations of the cross-validation of a model are definitely noisy, after all they are derived quantities calculated from the average of several individual observations (further averaged among folds). Therefore, a small change to the parameter can lead to a "relatively" large swing in observed value (your function is technically not continuous).

When doing bayesian optimization you are basically trying to fit a GP to the hyper-surface defined by f(ps) -> x. If this surface is noisy, your modelling of it should reflect it, it will lead to a better model that is more appropriate to the reality of your problem.

@GordonFreemem Thank you for the details.

> The data type on the SQLServer-side is date.

That's why dumpdata fails on your Linux machine. FreeTDS supports TDS protocol versions up to 7.2 and can't handle `date` type, and SQL Server converts a `date` value to a `varchar` value automatically when you retrieve it via FreeTDS. See this for the differences between TDS protocol versions:
http://www.freetds.org/userguide/choosingtdsprotocol.htm

But I wonder why the `date` type is assigned to the DateField because the backend usually assigns the legacy `datetime` type to a DateField when you run migrations using FreeTDS. Did you perhaps apply migrations from a Windows machine?

Per your question, the exp field is not automatically updated. There are a few reasons why this is the case. First, there is no way to update a JWT. The contents of the JWT (including the exp field) are all cryptographically signed based on the contents in the token. So if one field is changed in the JWT, the signed portion of the token would have to be updated as well, and you in essence end up with a whole new token.

Now, the reason this extension doesn't automatically send back new tokens is two fold. First, if the backend automatically refreshed a token whenever it was expired, that would mean that the frontend would need to be able to handle receiving a new token on any call to our API, regardless of if the call succeeded or failed. That is something that could be done, but it would require extra knowledge by users of our API, and if someone wasn't aware of this they could just keep using the same expired token forever (as the backend would keep creating new tokens for them), and a bunch of unused tokens would just be floating around. Not ideal. This would also limit what the API could return (it would always have to return a dict at the top level for example, as how could you return the new token to the caller if you were returning only a list?).

Second, if an access token was ever leaked, the person who acquires it would be able to keep generating new tokens and in essence stay logged in forever. As the access tokens are sent with every request, there is (at least in theory) more surface area where they could be leaked. If a refresh token is leaked, obviously we are in the same situation (except for the fact that refresh tokens also have an expires time on them, so eventually it will run out as well, but that may be a long time in the future). However, the refresh tokens should only be sent over the wire when requesting a new access token, so there should be less chance of them getting compromised.

So how can we use these refresh tokens on our frontend? In general there are two ways to go about this. The first way would be to set a timer to go off sometime before the access token expired (if the token expires after 5 minutes, maybe set a timer for 4 minutes) and once the timer hits 0 preemptively ask for a new access token using the refresh token. This has the benefit of being very simple, but has the drawbacks of potential breakage if your page is reload (assuming website frontend) and has some overlap where multiple tokens are floating around. The second way would be to use your access tokens as normal, and on every request check if you get an error back indicating that the token is expired. If you are doing a request and get an expired token error, fire off a request to refresh the access token, then re-make the original request (that had the expired token) using the newly received access token. This has the benefits of always working and only generating new tokens as needed, with the drawback of added complexity in your frontend code.

Phew! That was a wall of text. Hopefully this all makes sense and answers your question. If you want clarifications on any part or have any follow up questions, don't hesitate to let me know.

Cheers!

When I used `django-cassandra-engine` with `django-restframework` my silly solution was following. I made custom `serializers.Serializer` for cqlengine model with custom validations. For validating models I used python objects.

I will give an example as `Ticket` model.

This is `models.py` file:
```python
from cqlengine import columns
from cqlengine import models
from uuid import uuid1
from datetime import datetime


class Ticket(models.Model):
    __table_name__ = 'tickets'
    ticket_id = columns.TimeUUID(primary_key=True, default=uuid1())
    status = coumns.Integer(default=1, index=True)
    priority = columns.Integer(default=2, index=True)
    subject = columns.Text(required=True)
    content = columns.Text(required=True)
    created_at = columns.DateTime(default=datetime.now, index=True)

...
```

This is `objects.py` file:
```python
class TicketObject(object):                                                                                                                                                                  
    ticket_id = "" 
    status = 0
    priority = 2
    created_at = "" 


    def __init__(self, subject, content, **attrs):
        if 'status' in attrs:
            self.status = attrs['status']
        if 'priority' in attrs:
            self.priority = attrs['priority']
        self.subject = subject
        self.content = content
```

This is `serializers.py` file:
```python
from rest_framework import serializers
from tickets.objects import TicketObject


class TicketSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    ticket_id = serializers.CharField(required=False, read_only=True)
    status = serializers.IntegerField(required=False)
    priority = serializers.IntegerField(required=False)
    subject = serializers.CharField()
    content = serializers.CharField()
    created_at = serializers.DateTimeField(required=False, read_only=True)

    def restore_object(self, attrs, instance=None):
        if instance is not None:
            instance.status = attrs.get('status', instance.status)
            instance.priority = attrs.get('priority', instance.priority)
            instance.subject = attrs.get('subject', instance.subject)
            instance.content = attrs.get('content', instance.content)
            return instance
        return TicketObject(**attrs)
```

And for getting all these worked, I followed regular tutorials from django-restframework web-site to write my own customized `views`. 

This is small snippet of the `tickets/views.py`:
```python
from rest_framework.decorators import api_view, permission_classes
from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticated
from tickets.models import Ticket
from tickets.serializers import TicketSerializer
from rest_framework.response import Response

...

@api_view(['GET', 'POST'])
@permission_classes((IsAuthenticated,))
def all_tickets_collection(request):                                                                                                                                                           
    if request.method == 'GET':
        tickets = Ticket.objects.all()
        serializer = TicketSerializer(tickets, many=True)                                                                                                                                      
        return Response(serializer.data)
    elif request.method == 'POST':
        ticket = Ticket()
        serializer = TicketSerializer(data=request.DATA)
        if serializer.is_valid():
            try:
                i_need_some_token = my_custom_authenticate_function(request)
            except:
                return Response(
                    serializer.errors,
                    status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST
                )
            if serializer.data['status']:
                ticket.status = serializer.data['status']
            if serializer.data['priority']:
                ticket.priority = serializer.data['priority']
            ticket.subject = serializer.data['subject']
            ticket.content = serializer.data['content']
            
            ticket.save()
            return Response(
                {'status': 'Ticket created.'},
                status=status.HTTP_201_CREATED
            )

...
```

We think you are mentioning to the statement "Support for automating multiple apps in one session: No" in the link http://appium.io/slate/en/master/?javascript#appium-platform-support. 

The issue that we are trying to follow is as follows:

Let say we have an iOS Native App. It has a login screen and a Home screen (will be displayed once you successfully logged in). We have split these 2 screens (of the same app ) to 2 test scripts. We will execute the first test script using a pybot command. Once the test run is completed we would like to run the second test script (tap a button on the Home screen) using **another pybot command execution**.  
Now we haven't closed the app after the execution of the first script. So is there a way we can get the session id of the first test script run session  (using any keywords) and run the second test script on that session?
This will be a great option to have when we want to test an app with a lot of buttons and screens, want to split these into multiple scripts and want to run one test script after another using a Test management tool (like Testrail or testopia plugins), say iterate through the test cases in a test run (of the test management tool) and run one by one.

Performance test script:
import mxnet as mx

def benchmark_convolution(pass_type="whole", device_type="cpu", input_shape=None, kernel=None, stride=None, pad=None, cudnn_off=True):
    if input_shape is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Input shape must be (N, C, W) or (N, C, H, W) or (N, C, D, H, W")
    if kernel is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Kernel cannot be None and must match with dim of images")
    data = mx.sym.Variable(name="data")
    num_filter = 3
    if device_type == "cpu":
        dev = mx.cpu()
    else:
        dev = mx.gpu()
    conv = mx.sym.Convolution(data=data, kernel=kernel, stride=stride, pad=pad, num_filter=num_filter, no_bias="true",  cudnn_off=cudnn_off, name="conv")
    arg_shapes, out_shapes, _ = conv.infer_shape(data=input_shape)
    input_data = mx.random.uniform(-5, 5, input_shape, ctx=dev)
    out_grad = input_data
    args = {}
    args["data"] = input_data
    args["conv_weight"] = mx.random.normal(0, 1, (num_filter, input_shape[1]) + kernel, ctx=dev)
    args_grad = [mx.nd.empty(s) for s in arg_shapes]
    return mx.test_utils.check_speed(sym=conv, N=10, ctx=dev, typ=pass_type, data=input_shape, args=args, args_grad=args_grad)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print "v2, cpu, 1D: ", benchmark_convolution(input_shape=(10, 16, 512), kernel=(1,), stride=(1,), pad=(0,))
    print "v2, gpu, 1D: ", benchmark_convolution(device_type="gpu", input_shape=(10, 16, 512), kernel=(1,), stride=(1,), pad=(0,))
    print "\n"
    print "v2, cpu, 2D: ", benchmark_convolution(input_shape=(10, 16, 64, 64), kernel=(1, 1), stride=(1,1), pad=(0,0))
    print "v2, gpu, 2D: ", benchmark_convolution(device_type="gpu", input_shape=(10, 16, 64, 64), kernel=(1, 1), stride=(1,1), pad=(0,0))
    print "cudnn, gpu, 2D: ", benchmark_convolution(device_type="gpu", cudnn_off=False, input_shape=(10, 16, 64, 64), kernel=(1, 1), stride=(1,1), pad=(0,0))
    print "\n"
    print "v2, cpu, 3D: ", benchmark_convolution(input_shape=(4, 8, 10, 30, 20), kernel=(1, 1, 1), stride=(1,1,1), pad=(0,0,0))
    print "v2, gpu, 3D: ", benchmark_convolution(device_type="gpu", input_shape=(4, 8, 10, 30, 20), kernel=(1, 1, 1), stride=(1,1,1), pad=(0,0,0))
    print "cudnn, gpu, 3D: ", benchmark_convolution(device_type="gpu", cudnn_off=False, input_shape=(4, 8, 10, 30, 20), kernel=(1, 1, 1), stride=(1,1,1), pad=(0,0,0))

ports = []

    s = c.socket(zmq.SUB)

    for x in range(m):

I'm working from the Debian source so I can't just send a pull request but here is the diff I use to work around this issue: 
--- pyzmq-15.4.0.orig/zmq/eventloop/ioloop.py
+++ pyzmq-15.4.0/zmq/eventloop/ioloop.py
@@ -175,6 +175,16 @@ if (3, 0) <= tornado_version < (3, 1):
         return mini_loop.close.__get__(self)(all_fds)
     ZMQIOLoop.close = backport_close

+if (3, 0) <= tornado_version < (4, 0):
+    def backport_call_later(self, delay, callback, *args, **kwargs):
+        """backport IOLoop.call_later to 3.0 from 4.0"""
+        return self.call_at(self.time() + delay, callback, *args, **kwargs)
+    ZMQIOLoop.call_later = backport_call_later
+
+    def backport_call_at(self, when, callback, *args, **kwargs):
+        """backport IOLoop.call_at to 3.0 from 4.0"""
+        return self.add_timeout(when, callback, *args, **kwargs)
+    ZMQIOLoop.call_at = backport_call_at

 # public API name
 IOLoop = ZMQIOLoop

After some digging I discovered that django's InMemoryFileUpload object had a content_type property, but it was set to None. In boto (not boto3), we guess the content_type based on the file name, then use the default content_type. So in boto, the upload succeeds, because content_type is derrived from the name.


In boto3, it first pulls from a propery on the content called 'content_type'. If that property doesn't exist, it then falls to the type based on the name of the file. However, that fallback only occurs if the property doesn't exist. In django, it can exist and be None. I think boto3 should fallback if the property doesn't exist, or if it it does and it is None.

Yesterday the CDN provider on PyPI had a small disruption in the part of their service that handles ``PURGE`` requests. When the new version got uploaded to PyPI, and the PyPI backend software requested a ``PURGE`` of the currently cached entries, that purge didn't make it to every node in every PoP. This meant that some requests got served a stale response that didn't include the new release. This would have appeared to be an intermittent problem due to the nature of request routing within the CDN (you don't always get the same PoP or the same node within the PoP, and the differentiator between works or not is what PoP + node you were served by).

The disruption was quickly over, but it left any package that was uploaded in that time period in a potentially stale state. This would have fixed itself naturally over the course of a day or two as cached items fell out of the cache, however yesterday the PyPI admin team issued a manual purge to get everything back to 100%.

The reason that ClientData requires bytes (or base64 encoded data) is that the exact binary serialization used is important to maintain the sha256 digest. De-serializing this data, and then re-serializing it to get the raw bytes has a large chance of introducing changes in the data (for example the order of the fields, any whitespace changes, and so on), which would cause the digest to no longer match, and the signature validation to fail. For this reason, we don't allow it.

The VRF resources should be keyed by `VRF(rd='65000:1', name='Blue')` with a network in a VRF being keyed `Network(network='10.0.0.0', prefix_length=24, rd='65000:1')`

RD should always be 8 bytes and can be formatted in one of 3 ways:

* `<2-byte asn>:<4-byte value>`

From looking at the other `services`, @amishas157, it seems like we would want to do basic validation in the SDK *before* sending the request to the API. I'd follow a similar pattern to the other services, eg: https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/blob/master/mapbox/services/directions.py#L21

There does seem to be a catch-all for errors returned from the back-end service: https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/blob/master/mapbox/services/base.py#L68, but from my understanding, it should only fallback to this if there is some problem with the service (eg. a service outage), OR if there are parameters that we have no way of validating on the client-side (i.e. it requires some checking with a back-end data-store or so, for eg. whether a supplied valid looking token is actually valid). - We intended to, so we did a major version bump and still experienced backlash (we still have people on version 9 who expect / demand support)
- We did so accidentally (it was a bug) and experienced a flood of issues demanding to know why we don't announce and properly respect semantic versioning when breaking APIs

@kstrauser thanks for the report, but I'm not sure this is a bug...  When you unpin your Pipfile you are essentially giving the resolver free reign to figure out what version of any given package can fit within the constraints of the other packages in your environment.  That's why we recommend unpinning -- for multi-package environments, the resolver does the heavy-lifting of determining which packages require other packages to be specific versions.  If you unpin something, and then you re-resolve your dependencies (which is what locking does, and which happens whenever you install something because, well, dependencies will have to be resolved all over again), the resolver is going to re-evaluate your packages in the new context with the new install.

If the resolver finds that there is a package that can be upgraded (i.e. there is no other package in your environment holding it back, and you have unpinned it), it will go ahead and resolve it forward.  If you require specific versions of packages and you do not want the resolver to make that determination you will need to pin those.

As a sidenote, `--ignore-pipfile` is a meaningless option when you are installing a new package.  Anything that introduces something new to the environment can't ignore the pipfile, as it is writing to the pipfile.  The only way to avoid re-resolving the environment is to use `--skip-lock`, which also will not include the new package in the lockfile.  Similar to `--deploy`, `--ignore-pipfile` is meant when you are installing a pre-built lockfile into an environment (like a production environment for example) and you don't want any chance of the resolver taking the pipfile into consideration.

It was very counter-intuitive to have pytz and progressbar both working as expected, and upgrading setuptools broke pytz with a progressbar error despite being totally unrelated packages.  I'd like to do whatever we can to prevent unexpected coupling like that.

I think there's two issues leading to this unexpected behavior:

1. The import is doing nontrivial work that can raise all sorts of exceptions.  Looking at the pytz code, they are using `pkg_resources.resource_stream` to find an asset in pytz itself, and they want this to be a safe operation so they catch and handle ImportError.  They don't handle other exceptions---I think a very prevalent assumption is that imports only raise ImportErrors.
2. Whether or not this work happens on initialization, I also dont think that pkg_resources should crawl everything without being asked to.  Callers such as pytz are looking for something very specific, and pkg_resources doesn't need to probe everything in the universe to find it.  It should do something similar to the python import mechanism, which tries a bunch of candidate paths.

Looking at that original issue, I see that the WMI package has a setup.py with `import wmi` at the top level (in order to grab the `__version__` from that package)... and that's probably the behavior that was triggering the generation of caches at install time. I'd like to consider dropping support for that anti-pattern.

Looking at the latest WMI, it no longer imports wmi but instead does the following:

```
#
# Slight hack to avoid importing the module
# when setup is run
#
version = re.search (r'__version__ = "([a-z0-9\.]+)"', open ("wmi.py").read ()).group (1)
```

Although hacky, this approach is preferable to invoking behavior in dependencies, especially behavior that might write outside the sandbox.

Status update: with #273 merged and #238 mostly final, there are a few more things to do here.

- [ ] Make calls into the C module safe regardless the state of the objects; until now I got away with guarding against unsafe calls in the python wrapper classes, but the changes we made to BalancedConsumer (or perhaps just the extra tests we added, which trigger a lot of rebalancing and thus tear down the consumer repeatedly in quick succession) show that that's not good enough

- [ ] After merging master I should check that any newly added tests (particularly those from #269) also run against rdkafka. There's actually a bit of a design-smell here: every time we add tests to the suite, if we forgot to stick `use_rdkafka` flags in that test, it will only run against one or the other (it will run twice, but both times with the same backend - ugh).

The only way to guarantee strong producing order is to use a sync producer with strong ack setting. Send message get an ack from kafka and then send another message. This is supported in the current client but will be significantly slower then the async api.

Also note this is not a limitation of this client implementation. Enforcing strong ordering between to machines is a hard problem and this limitation will be present in all kafka clients.

path = sys.path

- having our conversion functions `img_as_float` clip to valid input rather than crashing and burning. (with or without a warning.)

Hi @sciunto 

> Is it still a work in progress?

The code itself is ready (needs a few adjustments as @grlee77 pointed) and the tasks in the message need to be worked on. Other than that, it is done.

> Could you explain in few words the differences between these two methods in practice, maybe with an example?

VisuShrink calculates the universal threshold based on the standard deviation of the diagonal detail coefficient, and being universal it is applied to all coefficients. This threshold is generally large and proportional to image size. The larger the image, more signal is killed along with noise: that leads to oversmoothing.

BayesShrink is subband adaptive and calculates a different threshold value for each detail coefficient. That means it has a better idea of what is noise and what is signal and denoising is more accurate to the original image.

I would show an image comparing both methods but apparently I need to upload it elsewhere then reference from Markdown.

Hi @rodrigo2019, and thanks for the PR!

Unfortunately, it doesn't actually implement what they propose in the paper:

> In other techniques like Sauvola’s and Niblack’s technique, local mean m(x,y) and standard
> deviation δ(x,y) are required to determine the value of the threshold for each pixel. In this
> proposed technique, no local standard deviation is required and it requires to compute the
> local mean and mean deviation ∂(x,y) to determine the local threshold
> [...]
> where ∂(x,y)=I(x,y) - m(x,y) is the local mean deviation and k is a bias which can control the
> level of adaptation varying threshold value.

Generally I think the paper is dishonest — for example, the "computational time" figure (3) appears to show Sauvola thresholding *without* using the integral image. Sauvola requires only one more pass over the image (to compute the deviation), so surely it must be within a factor of 2 or 3 of the proposed method, not 20x as they purport to show.

So, in my opinion both the method in the paper and the method implemented are unproven, and shouldn't be added to scikit-image. (There is a cost to increasing complexity.) I'm 👎 for merging. I'll let others weigh in though.

[synPermConnected, synPermConnected + 5 * synPermInactiveDec] for connected synapses and [synPermConnected - 5 * synPermActiveInc, synPermConnected] for unconnected synapses.

This doesn't remove the unused indices and aliases but it makes it a minor issue.

The blacklist issue is that new indices might still pop up when a module is updated.
This in fact breaks new builds as elasticsearch-dsl asks for `'elasticsearch>=5.0.0,<6.0.0'` and invenio-search asks for `'elasticsearch>=2.0.0,<3.0.0'`

I think the first of those two experiments is actually closer to what we want. Calling `_Command` multiple times should give the same result each time. It's _within_ a command that we want to avoid repeating component ids, since that's how we avoid the infinite loops I described.

I put together what I think is a complete implementation, though I haven't tested it:

```python
def _Commands(component, depth=3, avoid_cycles=True, used_ids=None):
  """Yields tuples representing commands for the component.
  
  Args:
    component: The component considered to be the root of the yielded commands.
    depth: The maximum depth with which to traverse the member DAG for commands.
        If depth is None, no depth constraints are imposed.
    used_ids: The set of the ids of the components already used in the command.
    avoid_cycles: If True, then commands will not repeat component ids they've
        already used.
  Yields:
    Tuples, each tuple representing one possible command for this component.
  """
  used_ids = used_ids or set()
  used_ids.add(id(component))

  if depth is not None and depth < 1:
    return
  next_depth = depth - 1 if depth is not None else None

  for member_name, member in _Members(component):
    if avoid_cycles and id(member) in used_ids:
      continue

    member_name = _FormatForCommand(member_name)
    yield (member_name,)
    
    if inspect.isroutine(member) or inspect.isclass(member):
      for completion in Completions(member):
        yield (member_name, completion)
      continue  # Don't descend into routines.
 
    for command in _Commands(member, used_ids, next_depth, avoid_cycles):
      yield (member_name,) + command
  
  used_ids.remove(id(component))
```

Calling `_Commands(component, depth=3, avoid_cycles=False)` should have the same behavior that the current `_Commands` implementation has, whereas calling `_Commands(component, depth=None, avoid_cycles=True)` should avoid repeating components within any command.

We'll have to add tests to verify this behavior before we're ready to check this or another implementation in.

What do you think?

Also, in Sparkling Water we use the IP address provided by Spark which in some environments is not what we want. We can override this by h2o client network & mask and h2o nodes network & mask. More info is here https://github.com/h2oai/sparkling-water/blob/master/doc/configuration/configuration_properties.rst . Look for `spark.ext.h2o.client.network.mask` for client node ( h2o running on spark driver ) and `spark.ext.h2o.node.network.mask` for h2o nodes in spark executors. So I would try to start H2OContext as this if the issue remains which sets these 2 configuration propetries:
```
val conf = new H2OConf(sparkSession).setClientNetworkMask("mask_for_h2o_client").setNodeNetworkMask("mask_for_h2o_nodes")
val h2oContext = H2OContext.getOrCreate(sparkSession, conf)
```

You can also use `setClienIP` which doesn't specify the network, but ip directly

After discussion and investigation w/ @wvangeit, it seems like the best course of action is:

1. Assume that SWC, when multiple points are specified, is a series of connected cylinders, with point locations at each x, y, z, and with radius defined by r
2. If there is a single point, assume it's a sphere with radius r, at x, y, z
3. If the point count is 3, see if it follows the pattern in http://neuromorpho.org/SomaFormat.html, and then interpret as 2 stacked cylinders with the point between them as 0, 0, 0
4. Raise a message if the parent ordering ordering isn't linear (NEURON drops these points, so we want to make people aware of that.

Finally, the area of the soma will be calculated as the area of the cylinders (without the end caps), and radius will be that of the equivalent sphere with that area.

In the example, I would expect that the user would need to add a 'DBZV' and 'DBZH' to the DEFAULT_FIELD_COLORMAP dictionary in order to have `get_field_colormap('DBZH')` and `get_field_colormap('DBZV')` return a meaningful colormap.  If these are not supplied I would expect the fields would be plotted using matplotlib's default colormap ('jet') which is what the `get_field_colormap` function will return if the field is not found in the DEFAULT_FIELD_COLORMAP dictionary.

Setting `reflectivity = 'DBZH' ` in the configuration file has no effect on the field names when reading netCDF files which are identified by Py-ART as being Cf/Radial format (most netCDF files) since Py-ART treats these files a bit difference then other files.  There are two reasons for this.  

First, Py-ART does no explicit renaming of field names when reading Cf/Radial files using the current default configuration, see the empty cfradial_field_mapping dictionary in in the default_configuration.py file.    

Second, unlike other read function read_cfradial does not exclude fields which are not mapped to a different name, rather the original field name from the file is used.  If this was not the case no fields would be returned in the radar object.  

An alternative to this is to explicitly provide a list of common field mappings the the cfradial_field_mapping dictionary.  This mapping would need to cover nearly all conceivable field names or else the field would not appear in the radar object which may not be obvious to the user.  In addition if multiple mapped fields are present in the file only the last one would make in into the radar object.  For example if 'DBZH' and 'reflectivity_horizontal' are both mapped to the reflectivity field a file containing both would overwrite one of these fields with data from the other.

If I recall this setup was implemented prior to the introduction of the **file_field_names** argument.  With this argument it may be reasonable to attempt to provide an explicit list of field mappings.  Perhaps with warnings when fields are skipping or overwritten this would be more appropriate.

like `package_data`, it should be a dictionary that maps from package names to lists of glob patterns.

@brandon-white that's because the root `setup.py` file is just a shell that points at each subpackage. `pip` then installs those from PyPI. What you really want to do is to clone the repo and then `pip install ${GIT_REPO}/bigtable/` (which will give you the `google-cloud-bigtable` repo). `pip` **may** allow you to point to subdirectories within a GitHub repo, but I think that is only allowed for the **local** `git` protocol supported by `pip`.

@lukesneeringer `annotate` isn't meant to be used directly. It should probably be named `_annotate` in hindsight. `detect()` is the documented method that calls `annotate()`.

That said, the signature change is required because multiple images need to be passed to `_vision_api.annotate()` in order to build the requests.

You build multiple `Request` objects and send them as a list and sending them as one request rather than making a request for each image.

Alternatively I could have duplicated the `annotate()` methods in `_http.py` and `_gax.py` and made the changes to handle multiple requests there, but there would have been a decent amount of duplicated code.

This PR is redundant to PR #4679 where PartialRowsData uses YieldRowsData, which in turn uses a generator, so the client can iterate over the rows on a large scan and not have to store them in a dictionary (taking lots of memory for large scans of 1M rows). If PartialRowsData is used, the generator stores the result in the **rows** dictionary, which already provides a built-in iterator, hence no need to add an iterator:
```
for row_key, row_data in partial_rows_data.rows.iteritems():
    ...
```
@sduskis has reviewed PR #4679 and participated in the design.  There is further refactoring of this  code in progress to improve performance of large scans and, since PartialRowData uses YieldRowsData in this PR, it will also benefit from this ongoing work.

If you're calling `@import 'bootstrap'` from another Sass file, you need to give it a path hint if it's bootstrap and your file aren't in the same directory. If you have something like 

```
Assets/
  _bootstrap.scss
  MyFiles/
      my_styles.scss
```

`my_styles.scss` would use `@import "..\bootstrap"`. If you include bootstrap in this way, then you only have to serve my_styles.css to the browser - bootstrap is included in that file now. 

`@import` in Sass is different than in Webpack - it can be confusing, but remember that all the `@import`s in a compiled scss file will be resolved and removed by the Sass compiler, and won't ever reach the browser. [Here's](http://vanseodesign.com/css/sass-the-import-directive/) a good resource on using @import in Sass.

@manasapte : The `scalastyle` and `checkstyle` tasks did not used to be installed by default, so it was necessary to install them via a custom plugin (ie, your own `register.py`). I expect that you have code that does that.

Okay, a few things:

* I don't believe there's a way to "join" a pull request with an issue. I think the best you can do is to add a link from each to the other so it's easy to navigate. It won't matter much, though, once we get a little further along, as all the conversation will be in this thread rather than the other.

* It's *really* important to get the rebase thing figured out so the changes you propose in any given commit reflect the final state of that code and are not reversed or modified in a subsequent commit. So if you change your mind about something, you go back to that commit, make the changes, amend the commit (`git commit -v --amend --reuse-message=HEAD`) and then rebase any later commits onto the one you've just amended.

  This gets really fast once you get the hang of it. What it produces is a successively refined set of "clean" commits that each accomplish a specific purpose. Each of these can be reviewed and merged individually as they pass muster. If you review historical commits you'll see this pattern over and over (except during some refactoring "periods"). A feature starts with a "docs: document XYZ analysis" commit, goes on to an "acpt: add scenarios for ..." acceptance test commit, then a series of individual property or method commits with its respective unit test. Each one is focused, relatively small (usually), and easy to validate.

  Otherwise, it's extremely difficult to see what the changes really are, because they're distributed across multiple commits. It would basically make it more work for the committer (me) than doing it myself.

  If you're a little bit wiggly on what rebasing is actually doing, I recommend this document: https://jwiegley.github.io/git-from-the-bottom-up/. It's the one that produced the "Aha!" moment for me way back when and delivered me into Git fluency (by understanding what I was doing, really, with each command).

Why an ignore tactic at all? In the BulidConfig.tactic method you could return None if the current entity is in the excludes list which won't include a tactic at all. This would be almost identical to the handling of the ignore list there which returns None if next layer would ignore this file. 

Its is very possible I am missing something, but at a glance

```
def tactic(self, entity, current, target, next_config):
        # Produce a tactic for the entity in question
        # These will be accumulate through the layers
        # and executed later
        bd = current.directory
        spec = pathspec.PathSpec.from_lines(pathspec.GitIgnorePattern,
                                            self.excludes)
        p = entity.relpath(bd)
        matches = spec.match_files((p,))
        if p in matches:
            return None

        # Ignore handling
        if next_config:
            spec = pathspec.PathSpec.from_lines(pathspec.GitIgnorePattern,
                                                next_config.ignores)
            p = entity.relpath(bd)
            matches = spec.match_files((p,))
            if p in matches:
                return None

        for tactic in self.tactics():
            if tactic.trigger(entity.relpath(bd)):
                return tactic(target=target, entity=entity,
                              current=current, config=next_config)
        return None


```
seems simpler

I foresee the following issues:
- Updating existing certs with attached templates... I've tried doing this through the UI and get some funky errors back that aren't obvious "Hey you can't change this while something else is using it".  The errors should be more obvious.
- Terminated HTTPS - Updating a cert where only the cert data and expiration date have changed (one of the most common use cases) should be seamless.  
- Non-terminated HTTPS: Example: member servers A, B, and D have been updated but C has not.   This means C must not be given any new connections until it can get the updated certificate.  How much of this needs to be handled in our software?

This should be ready for another round of reviews.  The drain now performs exactly as one would expect when the route to the remote end of the drain is interrupted.  With UDP, write errors go undetected and packets are spilled into the ether-- just the way UDP is supposed to work.  With TCP, the write errors also go more-or-less undetected, but that's because we don't reuse a connection for long enough for the OS' underlying TCP stack to give up on retrying writes and waiting for an ACK it will never get (this can actually take a _long_ time and Go doesn't allow timeouts to be set on individual writes).  However, by not using a connection for longer than a minute, the resulting redials can detect broken routes when the TCP handshake fails.  There would, thus, never be more than a one minute gap in logs before a warning about the broken route is recorded in the logger's own journal logs.

For example: I have `theano` installed from the `asmeurer` channel. Here's what currently happens:
```
0203-mgrant:~ mgrant$ conda update theano
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org
Fetching package metadata: ............
Error: No packages found in current osx-64 channels matching: theano
```
Here's what happens now:
```
0203-mgrant:conda mgrant$ conda update theano
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org
Fetching package metadata: ............
# All requested packages already installed.
# packages in environment at /Users/mgrant/miniconda2:
#
theano                    0.7.0                    py27_0    asmeurer
```
This could be improved, perhaps, by pulling down the entire index for that channel, and adding those files to the index.
